story.title: Dr. Hassan's Guide to the Cell's Energy Factory
story.text:
Hello, I am Dr. Amina Hassan. I study the tiny factories inside your body’s cells. These factories are always busy. They keep you alive!

The main engine in each factory is the mitochondria. It needs two things to work. It needs nutrients from food and oxygen from air. The mitochondria combine these inputs to make energy. This process is called cellular respiration.

The energy it makes is a molecule called ATP. Your cells use ATP for everything. They use it to grow and make new parts. We call this the cell growth rate. More energy means a faster growth rate. But the factory also creates trash. This is called metabolic waste. If waste builds up, it slows the factory down. My research helps us understand how to keep these factories running well to fight disease.
think_abouts: What are the two main 'ingredients' a cell's mitochondria need to make energy?
What happens to a cell's growth rate if its mitochondria make less ATP?
story.go_deeper: During hard exercise, your muscles 'burn'. How might 'oxygen debt' and a buildup of 'metabolic waste' cause this feeling?

margin_defs.definition: the power to do work or grow

check_prompts: What is the name of the cell's 'engine' that makes energy?

vocabulary.definition: The part of the cell, known as the 'power plant', that makes ATP energy.
vocabulary.use_sentence: The mitochondria are the tiny engines inside our cells.

vocabulary.definition: The special molecule that carries energy for all of the cell's activities.
vocabulary.use_sentence: A cell needs a lot of ATP to grow and divide.

vocabulary.definition: The process that uses oxygen to turn food into ATP energy inside mitochondria.
vocabulary.use_sentence: Cellular respiration gives our bodies the power they need.

vocabulary.definition: The leftover materials from cell processes, like carbon dioxide, that must be removed.
vocabulary.use_sentence: Our blood carries metabolic waste away from our cells.

vocabulary.definition: A measure of how fast a cell can make new parts and divide.
vocabulary.use_sentence: The cell growth rate depends on how much ATP is available.

discussion_cards.prompt: Imagine you are running a race. What happens to the amount of nutrients and oxygen your muscle cells need? How does this change the mitochondria's activity?
discussion_cards.frame: When I run a race, my cells need more ___. This makes the mitochondria work ___ to produce more ___.

discussion_cards.prompt: Dr. Hassan studies diseases where mitochondria do not work well. What do you predict happens to a cell's growth rate and the amount of waste if its mitochondria are broken?
discussion_cards.frame: If a cell's mitochondria are broken, I predict the cell growth rate will ___ because ___.

reflection_frames.knew: Before reading, I already knew that ___
reflection_frames.learned: From the story, I learned that ___
